nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated hill streams and mountain rivers with rocky substrates, cobble, and gravel; clear, cool water with strong current

decorPreferences: smooth river cobbles and water-worn boulders
flat rocks and slate pieces creating caves and overhangs
Anubias and Microsorum tied to hardscape (robust plants tolerating high flow)

diet.notes: Aufwuchs grazer and opportunistic insectivore in the wild; rasps biofilm, aufwuchs, and small invertebrates from rock surfaces in fast-flowing streams. In captivity readily accepts sinking catfish pellets, micro-granules, and algae wafers as staple; supplement with frozen or live bloodworms, white mosquito larvae, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and daphnia. Occasional blanched vegetable matter (zucchini, cucumber) accepted. Feed small portions 1–2 times daily; avoid over-feeding in high-flow setups.

goodWith: hillstream loaches (Gastromyzontidae) in large setups with ample territory
avoidWith: small groups of conspecifics (3–9 individuals) — causes severe intraspecific aggression and specimen loss
slow-moving, placid fish unable to avoid harassment
very small nano fish that may be bullied or eaten

summary: The Crimson Loach is a striking, undescribed Schistura species traded under a color-form name, most likely originating from fast hill streams in Kerala (India) or possibly Myanmar. Its vivid crimson-and-dark-banded pattern, high-flow demands, and intense territorial behavior make it a specialist’s fish requiring a mature, strongly oxygenated stream biotope.
careNotes:
The Crimson Loach (Schistura sp. ‘Crimson’) is one of the most visually striking members of the sprawling, largely undescribed Schistura genus. It remains scientifically undescribed as of 2026 and is known only by its trade designation; documented specimens have come from Nilambur, Kerala, southern India, though some trade stock may originate from Myanmar or adjacent SE Asian stream systems.

This species demands hillstream conditions: powerful filtration (turnover 10×+ per hour), strong directional flow, and near-saturated dissolved oxygen. It is highly sensitive to nitrate accumulation; weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are strongly advised. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (gH 2–12, kH 1–6, pH 6.0–7.5) at cool-to-moderate tropical temperatures (22–26°C) best replicates its native hill-stream habitat. Temperatures above 27°C stress this species.

Territoriality is intense, particularly among males. The safest stocking options are a single specimen, a known compatible pair, or a dense group of 10 or more in a large aquarium (≥2× the footprint minimum) with abundant visual barriers between territories. Keeping 3–9 fish in a standard tank reliably results in specimen losses. Tankmates should be active, current-loving fish of a similar size that will not be out-competed for food at the bottom; Danio, Rasbora, and small Barb species are commonly recommended dither fish. Avoid other nemacheilid loaches entirely.

As an aufwuchs grazer, this species benefits from a mature, well-established aquarium with biofilm on rocks and hardscape. Supplement with high-quality sinking foods and regular frozen/live invertebrate offerings. A tight-fitting lid is recommended; like most loaches, it can escape through small gaps.

Breeding has not been achieved in home aquaria as of 2026. Sexual dimorphism is unknown; intensity of red coloration may correlate with sex or dominance status.
breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity as of 2026. As an undescribed species from fast hill-stream environments, it presumably requires cool, highly oxygenated running water to trigger spawning, likely tied to seasonal flow and temperature variation. No records of egg or fry observations exist in the hobby literature. Sexual dimorphism is undocumented; mature females of related Schistura species are typically more robust-bodied than males.

primary.name: Loaches Online — Schistura sp. “Crimson”
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided origin (Kerala, Nilambur, Southern India), adult size (~10 cm), water parameters (23–26°C, pH 6–7.5, hardness max 12 dH), hillstream habitat requirements, diet (pellets, frozen invertebrates), behavior (territorial, nippy toward conspecifics), compatibility (danio/rasbora/barb dither fish), stocking advice (solo, pair, or 10+), common name ‘Jasper Loach’, and note that breeding is unknown. Species was photographed from Kerala stock.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Crimson Loach, Schistura sp. Crimson
additional.notes: Confirmed hillstream care requirements, territorial behavior, stocking guidelines (solo/pair/10+), and diet. HTTP 403 on direct fetch; data cross-checked via aggregated search summaries citing this source.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Crimson Loach (Schistura crimson)
additional.notes: Confirmed origin (Nilambur, Kerala), adult size (10 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.2, hardness up to 12 dH, diet (catfish pellets, granules, flake, frozen bloodworm/mosquito larvae/brineshrimp/daphnia), high-flow hillstream requirements, and confirmation that species has not been bred in home aquaria.

additional.name: AquariumStockingCalculator — Crimson Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: Corroborated temperature range 22–25°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank 80 L (21 gal), adult size 8 cm, omnivorous diet (invertebrates, detritus, sinking pellets, frozen foods), and active bottom-dwelling behavior in fast-flowing rocky environments.

additional.name: Tropical Live Fish Store — Crimson Loach
additional.notes: Provided lifespan estimate of 6 years and confirmed Southern India origin. Notes territorial intraspecific behavior settling once territories are established. Diet listed as flake and sinking pellet.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding records as of 2026. Spawning likely requires cool, highly oxygenated flowing-water conditions and possibly seasonal temperature or flow triggers replicating monsoon stream conditions. Sexual dimorphism unknown.
